reason: RQ1: extends — difficulty-aware dynamic workflow generation selects multi-agent configurations per task, relevant to adaptive task dispatch in decision agent teams (WWW2026).

RQ1: extends — Orchestrated Distributed Intelligence reconceptualizes multi-agent AI as cohesive orchestrated networks with multi-loop feedback, a potential paradigm-level reframe of decision agent design.
RQ1: extends — state-aware routing encodes interaction history and agent knowledge to select the most suitable worker at each step, directly addressing worker selection in decision agent teams.
RQ3: extends — evaluates plan adherence fidelity in agentic systems, directly relevant to whether decision agent can independently close the loop without long-term assistant context.
RQ1: extends — hierarchical agent trees decompose complex goals into subgoals with control flow, an alternative architectural pattern for multi-agent workers team task decomposition.
RQ2: extends — cross-team orchestration mechanism addresses how a manager-level scheduler (openclaw) coordinates multiple decision agent worker teams.
RQ2: extends — context engineering for corporate multi-agent architecture is directly relevant to how openclaw maintains and passes long-horizon context as manager above decision agent.
RQ1: extends — unified orchestration layer integrating MCP and A2A protocols addresses coordination infrastructure for multi-agent decision systems.
RQ1: extends — taxonomy of agentic AI architectures covering the transition from single-agent loops to organized multi-agent systems provides landscape context for decision agent positioning.
RQ1: extends — comprehensive survey of multi-agent collaboration mechanisms covering actor types, structures, strategies, and coordination protocols provides foundational landscape.
RQ1: extends — 2024 general survey on LLM multi-agents, superseded by more recent and focused surveys; no methodological contribution on decision agent architecture.
RQ1: extends — general LLM agent methodology survey with broad scope, tangential to the specific multi-agent workers team architecture of the decision agent.
RQ1: orthogonal — domain-specific application of multi-agent orchestration to IT incident response; architectural insights not transferable to general decision agent design.
RQ1: orthogonal — multi-agent framework narrowly scoped to agile software engineering tasks within a single domain, not general decision agent architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — focuses on user personalization and preference learning for single-agent systems, not multi-agent team coordination or decision agent architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — survey focuses on RL training methods for individual LLMs, not on deployment-time multi-agent decision architectures or team coordination.
no RQ: orthogonal — evaluation methodology survey for LLM agents, tangential to architectural questions in RQ1–3 about team coordination and standalone closure.
RQ1: extends — general agentic LLM survey without focus on multi-agent team coordination; superseded by more targeted sources already in the skim list.
no RQ: orthogonal — practitioner best-practices guide for production agentic workflows, not a research contribution to multi-agent decision architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — meta-tool optimization addresses redundant tool-call patterns in single-agent workflows, not multi-agent team coordination or decision architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — formal specification language for agent resource constraints; not directly addressing multi-agent team coordination or decision architecture.
no RQ: reject — agentic RAG focuses on retrieval-augmented generation pipelines; no relevance to multi-agent team coordination or decision agent architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — focuses on RL training methods for multi-agent coordination games, not on LLM-based decision agent architectural design for end-to-end task completion.
RQ1: extends — cooperative behavior profiles predict multi-agent team performance, informing agent selection and composition for decision agent worker teams.
RQ1: extends — role-based author-reviewer-metareviewer hierarchy achieves 50% efficiency gain over multi-agent debate, relevant to role design in decision agent worker teams.
RQ1: extends — systematic analysis of 94 papers identifying role-based cooperation as dominant MAS design pattern, providing actionable design guidance for decision agent worker architecture.
RQ1: extends — IEEE-published survey of agent workflow architectures including multi-agent collaboration patterns and orchestration strategies, contextualizing decision agent design.
RQ1: extends — autonomous multi-agent optimization via iterative refinement feedback loops (Refinement, Execution, Evaluation agents), modeling a self-improving decision agent worker team.
RQ1: extends — structured taxonomy of multi-agent communication dimensions (who, what, when, where, why) informs protocol design for decision agent worker team coordination.
RQ1: extends — foundational two-stage team optimization and task-solving framework (DyLAN), provides historical baseline for dynamic decision agent worker team assembly.
RQ1: extends — enumerates open challenges in LLM multi-agent systems including collective reasoning and safety, framing the research gaps decision agent must address.
RQ3: orthogonal — reframes agent evaluation around financial ROI rather than capability; tangentially relevant to standalone decision agent deployment viability and adoption.
no RQ: orthogonal — interpretability methods for agentic systems; tangential to RQ1 via understanding agent decision processes, but no direct architecture or collaboration contribution.
no RQ: reject — applies MAS to social science simulation; no decision agent architecture or worker team methodology applicable to the thesis.
no RQ: reject — data systems and serving infrastructure perspective on agentic workflows; no contribution to decision agent design, collaboration, or standalone operation.
no RQ: reject — formal verification of agent safety and security properties; skim budget exhausted by higher-relevance candidates; no architecture or collaboration methodology.

RQ1: extends — co-evolving decision agent + learnable skill bank achieves autonomous long-horizon task completion, directly modeling multi-agent worker architecture with self-improving skill discovery.
RQ2: extends — write-manage-read memory taxonomy covering personal assistants and collaborative teams; foundational to how openclaw provides persistent context feeding the decision agent.

RQ1: extends — MCTS-based dynamic orchestration with dual-memory (working + experience) as end-to-end task completion mechanism; novel orchestration pattern though domain-specific to clinical reasoning.

RQ1/RQ3: extends — DAG-based execution harness (SGH) with immutable plans and separated planning/recovery layers; position paper identifying structural weaknesses in agent loops relevant to decision agent reliability.
RQ1: extends — maps LLM-agent architectures to classical planning methodologies; web-specific but planning taxonomy applicable to decision agent task decomposition.
RQ1: extends — studies emergent coordination behavior in multi-agent LLM systems without explicit orchestration design; foundational for understanding organic dynamics in workers-team.
RQ1: extends — LLM high-level planning with MARL for multi-agent coordination; tangentially relevant to how decision agent orchestrates workers via learned policies.

RQ1: extends — non-parametric cross-episode decision evolution via natural-language playbook; mechanism applicable to decision agent skill adaptation but domain-specific to spacecraft (CVPR 2026 AI4Space Workshop).
no RQ direct match: orthogonal — Bayesian belief aggregation across LLMs for domain-specific cost-aware decisions; shares multi-LLM orchestration vocabulary but not workers-team architecture design.
no RQ direct match: orthogonal — evaluation/governance synthesis framework identifying a governance-to-action closure gap; no architectural contribution to decision agent design.
RQ1: extends — 2024 survey of agentic workflow components and LLM profiling; foundational context but predates current landscape coverage and adds no new contribution.

no RQ: reject — agentic workflow serving and batching optimization; no architectural contribution to decision agent design or multi-agent coordination.

RQ1: extends — graph-based agent selection (node sampling) and bidirectional message passing as coordination topology for multi-agent workers team; ICLR 2026 accepted, strong lab pedigree (James Zou, Guohao Li, Tianlong Chen).

RQ1: extends — systematic survey of LLM agent optimization (RL, fine-tuning, prompt engineering) published in ACM Computing Surveys; incremental relative to existing RL coverage in the landscape but high-gravity reference.

RQ1: extends — advocates BDI architectures, communication protocols, and mechanism design as principled foundations for agentic AI; foundational position relevant to decision agent design but no empirical contribution.
RQ1: orthogonal — LLM-derived graph priors enhance MARL coordination in particle environments; cooperative MARL setting, not LLM-based decision agent workers team for end-to-end task completion.

RQ1: extends — hierarchical temporal logic for LLM-driven multi-agent task planning; robotics domain limits transferability but hierarchical task decomposition mechanism tangentially relevant to decision agent planning.

RQ1: orthogonal — empirical study of development patterns and coordination issues across 8 open-source multi-agent frameworks; practical engineering insights without architectural methodology contribution.
RQ2: orthogonal — uses uncertainty as a planning signal in multi-turn goal-oriented conversation; conversational AI domain, not multi-agent task execution or openclaw-style orchestration.
no RQ: orthogonal — conceptual taxonomy distinguishing AI agents from agentic AI; definitional framing with no methodological contribution to decision agent or workers team design.
no RQ: reject — analyzes agentic AI execution from a CPU and hardware performance perspective; no agent architecture or decision-making methodology relevant to any RQ.

no RQ: reject — organizational guide for adopting agentic AI addressing change management and human orchestration roles; no technical methodology for decision agent architecture or workers team design.
no RQ: reject — multi-robot physical collaboration via task-level similarity memoization; robotics execution domain with no overlap to LLM-based decision agent workers team architecture.

RQ1,RQ3: extends — identifies step-wise greedy reasoning as fundamental planning failure for long-horizon tasks; FLARE's future-aware lookahead directly addresses how decision agents achieve end-to-end task closure and standalone operation.
